critic_raw:
The preprocessing explicitly fits all statistics on the full 24-month dataset: 'all preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are computed using the entire dataset before any splitting occurs.' For temporal data, this is chronological leakage: the normalization parameters for Year 1 training features are computed using Year 2 operational data including the test period.

The RL environment simulates daily routing, but the reward function includes α, β, γ, δ as tunable hyperparameters that are grid-searched on the validation set. This means the reward function itself—not just the policy—is tuned to validation outcomes. An RL agent whose reward weights are optimized on the validation set is not demonstrating that RL learns good routing policy; it is demonstrating that the reward can be tuned to match a specific validation period. The generalization claim requires that the reward function be fixed before training, not optimized alongside the policy.

The 'traditional nearest-neighbor heuristic with 2-opt local optimization' is described as the baseline representing 'current operational standard.' But 2-opt local search is only approximately optimal; for large routing instances, it can be far from optimal and is not representative of state-of-the-art heuristics (e.g., Lin-Kernighan, LKH3, or OR-Tools). The choice of a weak heuristic baseline lowers the bar the RL agent must clear and inflates the apparent improvement.

The customer sentiment signal (derived from a 'pre-trained sentiment model to extract customer satisfaction signals without retraining') introduces a confound: the sentiment model may have its own systematic biases (e.g., sensitivity to specific phrasing, demographic effects) that are unrelated to actual delivery performance. Including sentiment as a feature and also as part of the reward penalty creates a circularity where the model learns to optimize the sentiment model's outputs rather than actual customer satisfaction.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing statistics including normalization parameters and demand aggregation quantiles are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, leaking Year 2 test-period data into the Year 1 training feature representations.

The RL reward function weights (α, β, γ, δ) are tuned on the validation set alongside policy hyperparameters, meaning the reward function itself is optimized post-hoc rather than being specified a priori; this conflates reward engineering with policy learning.

The nearest-neighbor with 2-opt baseline is a weak representative of traditional heuristics; compared to standard solvers like LKH3 or OR-Tools, it sets an artificially low performance bar that inflates the apparent RL advantage.

Customer sentiment derived from a pre-trained model is used as both a feature and a reward penalty; if the sentiment model has systematic biases unrelated to actual satisfaction, the RL agent learns to optimize the sentiment model's artifacts rather than true delivery quality.

The nearest-neighbor with 2-opt baseline is a weak representative of traditional heuristics; compared to standard solvers like LKH3 or OR-Tools, it sets an artificially low performance bar that inflates the apparent RL advantage.
